file.content:
---
description: "Archives a completed feature to docs/evolution/ and cleans up workflow files. Use after all implementation steps pass and mutation testing completes."
disable-model-invocation: true
argument-hint: '[agent] [project-id] - Example: @platform-architect "auth-upgrade"'
---

# NW-FINALIZE: Feature Completion and Archive

**Wave**: CROSS_WAVE
**Agent**: @nw-platform-architect (default) or specified agent

## Overview

Finalize a completed feature: verify all steps done|create evolution document in docs/evolution/|clean up workflow files in docs/feature/{project-id}/|optionally generate reference docs. Agent gathers project data|analyzes execution history|writes summaries|archives|cleans up.

## Usage

```
/nw:finalize @{agent} "{project-id}"
```

## Context Files Required

- docs/feature/{project-id}/roadmap.yaml - Original project plan
- docs/feature/{project-id}/execution-log.yaml - Step execution history (Schema v2.0)

## Pre-Dispatch Gate: All Steps Complete

Before dispatching, verify all steps are done — prevents archiving incomplete features.

Parse execution-log.yaml, verify every step has status DONE. If any step is not DONE, block finalization and list incomplete steps with current status. Do not dispatch until all steps complete.

## Agent Invocation

@{agent}

Finalize: {project-id}

**Key constraints:**
- Create evolution document in docs/evolution/YYYY-MM-DD-{project-id}.md
- Archive workflow files, do not delete before user approval
- Verify all steps DONE before proceeding
- Update architecture doc statuses from "FUTURE DESIGN" to "IMPLEMENTED"
- Optionally invoke /nw:document for reference docs (skip with --skip-docs)
- Commit and push evolution document after approval

## Phases

Agent handles: gather project data|analyze completion stats|write evolution document|archive to docs/evolution/|clean up workflow files (after user approval)|update architecture docs|commit.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All steps verified DONE before dispatch
- [ ] Evolution document created in docs/evolution/
- [ ] User approved summary before cleanup
- [ ] Workflow directory cleaned up
- [ ] Architecture docs updated to "IMPLEMENTED" status
- [ ] Evolution document committed and pushed

## Error Handling

| Error | Response |
|-------|----------|
| Invalid agent name | "Invalid agent. Available: nw-researcher, nw-software-crafter, nw-solution-architect, nw-product-owner, nw-acceptance-designer, nw-platform-architect" |
| Missing project ID | "Usage: /nw:finalize @agent 'project-id'" |
| Project directory not found | "Project not found: docs/feature/{project-id}/" |
| Incomplete steps | Block finalization, list incomplete steps |

## Examples

### Example 1: Standard finalization
```
/nw:finalize @nw-platform-architect "auth-upgrade"
```
Verifies all steps done, invokes nw-platform-architect with "Finalize: auth-upgrade". Agent reads project files, creates docs/evolution/2026-02-08-auth-upgrade.md, requests cleanup approval, commits.

### Example 2: Architect summary
```
/nw:finalize @nw-solution-architect "microservices-migration"
```

<!-- DES-VALIDATION : required -->
<!-- DES-PROJECT-ID : workspace-repo-path -->
<!-- DES-STEP-ID : 01-03 -->

# DES_METADATA
Step: 01-03
Project: workspace-repo-path
Command: /nw:execute

# AGENT_IDENTITY
Agent: nw-functional-software-crafter

# SKILL_LOADING
Before starting TDD phases, read your skill files for methodology guidance.
Skills path: ~/.claude/skills/nw/nw-functional-software-crafter/
Always load at PREPARE: tdd-methodology.md, quality-framework.md
Load on-demand per phase as specified in your Skill Loading Strategy table.

# TASK_CONTEXT
Step: 01-03
Name: Workspace creation with optional repo_path
Description: Wire repoPath through workspace creation flow: parse from request body, validate if present, persist to workspace record.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Workspace created with valid repoPath persists it and returns in bootstrap
- Workspace created with invalid repoPath is rejected with 400
- Workspace created without repoPath succeeds; bootstrap omits repoPath

Dependencies: 01-01 (schema+contracts), 01-02 (validateRepoPath function + repo-path endpoint) — both complete
Time Estimate: 20 min

Acceptance Tests: tests/acceptance/coding-agent-orchestrator/repo-path.test.ts
Relevant scenarios (already written):
- Scenario 3: Persist repo_path on creation (line 132)
- Scenario 4: Reject invalid repo_path on creation (line 152)
- Scenario 5: Allow creation without repo_path (line 172)

Key Files to examine/modify:
- app/src/server/workspace/workspace-routes.ts — modify workspace creation handler to parse repoPath, validate with validateRepoPath if present, persist to workspace record
- app/src/server/http/parsing.ts — check if workspace creation parsing needs repoPath
- app/src/shared/contracts.ts — CreateWorkspaceRequest already has repoPath?: string (step 01-01)

Important project rules (from AGENTS.md):
- Never use null. Use undefined via optional properties
- Use RecordId objects, not string-based record IDs
- Functional paradigm — pure functions, pipeline composition
- Fail fast: throw immediately when required data is missing
- Follow existing route patterns for endpoint registration

Prior step context: Step 01-02 added validateRepoPath function and POST /api/workspaces/:ws/repo-path endpoint. The validateRepoPath function is already available to reuse for creation-time validation.

# TDD_PHASES
Execute in order:
0. PREPARE - Read workspace-routes.ts to understand current creation flow. Read acceptance test scenarios 3-5.
1. RED_ACCEPTANCE - Acceptance tests exist. Log as SKIPPED (pre-written in DISTILL wave).
2. RED_UNIT - If creation flow has testable pure functions, write unit tests. Otherwise SKIP with reason.
3. GREEN - Modify workspace creation handler: parse repoPath from body, call validateRepoPath if present (reject 400 if invalid), include repo_path in CREATE query. Ensure bootstrap endpoint returns repoPath.
   After GREEN: run FULL test suite. If all pass, proceed to COMMIT immediately.
4. COMMIT - Stage and commit with conventional message
   Include git trailer: `Step-ID: 01-03`

# RECORDING_INTEGRITY
- Only record phases you actually executed
- Never fabricate timestamps or phase data
- Use the DES CLI for all recording — do not manually edit execution-log.yaml
- A log entry without actual execution is fraud
- If a phase is skipped, use status SKIPPED with a genuine reason

# QUALITY_GATES
- All tests pass before COMMIT
- No skipped phases without blocked_by reason
- Coverage maintained or improved

# OUTCOME_RECORDING
After ACTUALLY EXECUTING each phase, record via DES CLI:

    PYTHONPATH=$HOME/.claude/lib/python python -m des.cli.log_phase \
      --project-dir docs/feature/workspace-repo-path \
      --step-id 01-03 \
      --phase {PHASE_NAME} \
      --status EXECUTED \
      --data PASS

For SKIPPED phases:

    PYTHONPATH=$HOME/.claude/lib/python python -m des.cli.log_phase \
      --project-dir docs/feature/workspace-repo-path \
      --step-id 01-03 \
      --phase {PHASE_NAME} \
      --status SKIPPED \
      --data "NOT_APPLICABLE: reason"

# BOUNDARY_RULES
- Only modify files related to this step
- Do not load roadmap.yaml
- Do not modify execution-log.yaml structure (append only via CLI)

# TIMEOUT_INSTRUCTION
Target: 30 turns max. If approaching limit, COMMIT current progress.
If GREEN complete (all tests pass), MUST commit before returning.

        for j in range(start, end):
            print(f'{j}: {lines[j]}')
        print('---')

1→"""Session Guard Policy - blocks source writes during deliver without DES.
     2→
     3→Pure domain policy that prevents the main agent from writing to source/test
     4→files during a deliver session unless a DES-monitored subagent is currently
     5→running. Orchestration artifacts (docs/feature/, .nwave/, .develop-progress)
     6→are always allowed.
     7→
     8→This closes Scenario B: the main agent implementing steps directly with
     9→Read/Write/Edit instead of delegating to a software-crafter via Task.
    10→"""
    11→
    12→from __future__ import annotations
    13→
    14→from dataclasses import dataclass
    15→
    16→
    17→@dataclass(frozen=True)
    18→class GuardResult:
    19→    """Result of a session guard check.
    20→
    21→    Attributes:
    22→        blocked: True if the write should be blocked
    23→        reason: Block reason if blocked, None otherwise
    24→    """
    25→
    26→    blocked: bool
    27→    reason: str | None = None
    28→
    29→
    30→class SessionGuardPolicy:
    31→    """Guards source file writes during deliver sessions.
    32→
    33→    Protected paths: src/, tests/ (must go through DES subagent)
    34→    Allowed paths: docs/feature/, .nwave/, .develop-progress (orchestration)
    35→    """
    36→
    37→    PROTECTED_PATTERNS = ["src/", "tests/"]
    38→
    39→    ALLOWED_PATTERNS = [
    40→        "docs/feature/",
    41→        ".nwave/",
    42→        ".develop-progress",
    43→    ]
    44→
    45→    def check(
    46→        self,
    47→        file_path: str,
    48→        session_active: bool,
    49→        des_task_active: bool,
    50→    ) -> GuardResult:
    51→        """Check if a file write should be allowed.
    52→
    53→        Args:
    54→            file_path: Path of the file being written
    55→            session_active: True if a deliver session is active
    56→            des_task_active: True if a DES-monitored subagent is running
    57→        """
    58→        if not session_active:
    59→            return GuardResult(blocked=False)
    60→
    61→        if any(p in file_path for p in self.ALLOWED_PATTERNS):
    62→            return GuardResult(blocked=False)
    63→

    66→

store now tracks repoPath from bootstrap response.

# DES_METADATA
Step: 01-04
Project: workspace-repo-path
Command: /nw:execute

# AGENT_IDENTITY
Agent: nw-functional-software-crafter

# SKILL_LOADING
Before starting TDD phases, read your skill files for methodology guidance.
Skills path: ~/.claude/skills/nw/nw-functional-software-crafter/
Always load at PREPARE: tdd-methodology.md, quality-framework.md
Load on-demand per phase as specified in your Skill Loading Strategy table.

# TASK_CONTEXT
Step: 01-04
Name: Assignment guard and orchestrator wiring
Description: Add REPO_PATH_REQUIRED check to assignment guard. Remove static repoRoot from OrchestratorWiringDeps; read repo_path from workspace record at assignment time and pass to session lifecycle.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Task assignment on workspace without repo_path returns 400 JSON { error: <string> } where error contains "repo_path"
- Task assignment succeeds after repo_path is set on workspace
- OrchestratorWiringDeps no longer accepts static repoRoot; repo_path resolved per-workspace from DB at assignment time
- Session lifecycle receives repo_path from workspace record, not process.cwd()

Dependencies: 01-01 (schema), 01-02 (validation+endpoint), 01-03 (creation flow) — all complete
Time Estimate: 30 min

Acceptance Tests: tests/acceptance/coding-agent-orchestrator/repo-path.test.ts
Relevant scenarios (already written):
- Scenario 1: Block assignment without repo_path (line 68) — expects 400 with body.error containing "repo_path"
- Scenario 2: Allow assignment after repo_path set (line 94) — expects 200 with agentSessionId

Key Files to examine/modify:
- app/src/server/coding-agent-orchestrator/assignment-guard.ts — add REPO_PATH_REQUIRED check
- app/src/server/coding-agent-orchestrator/session-lifecycle.ts — use repo_path from workspace instead of static repoRoot
- app/src/server/coding-agent-orchestrator/routes.ts — remove static repoRoot from deps, resolve per-workspace
- app/src/server/runtime/start-server.ts — check how repoRoot is passed to orchestrator wiring, may need to remove it
- app/src/server/runtime/dependencies.ts — check if repoRoot comes from here

Important project rules (from AGENTS.md):
- Never use null. Use undefined via optional properties
- Use RecordId objects, not string-based record IDs
- Functional paradigm — pure functions, pipeline composition
- Fail fast: throw immediately when required data is missing
- Follow existing assignment guard patterns for the new check
- The assignment guard already has checks for workspace membership and task status — add REPO_PATH_REQUIRED between them

# TDD_PHASES
Execute in order:
0. PREPARE - Read assignment-guard.ts, session-lifecycle.ts, routes.ts, start-server.ts. Understand how repoRoot flows currently. Read acceptance test scenarios 1-2.
1. RED_ACCEPTANCE - Acceptance tests exist. Log as SKIPPED (pre-written in DISTILL wave).
2. RED_UNIT - Write unit tests for the assignment guard's new REPO_PATH_REQUIRED check if the guard has unit tests. Check tests/unit/ for existing guard tests.
3. GREEN - Add REPO_PATH_REQUIRED check to assignment guard. Modify orchestrator wiring to resolve repo_path per-workspace from DB. Remove static repoRoot from OrchestratorWiringDeps. Pass resolved repo_path to session lifecycle.
   After GREEN: run FULL test suite (including acceptance tests). If all pass, proceed to COMMIT.
4. COMMIT - Stage and commit with conventional message
   Include git trailer: `Step-ID: 01-04`

# RECORDING_INTEGRITY
- Only record phases you actually executed
- Never fabricate timestamps or phase data
- Use the DES CLI for all recording — do not manually edit execution-log.yaml
- A log entry without actual execution is fraud
- If a phase is skipped, use status SKIPPED with a genuine reason

# QUALITY_GATES
- All tests pass before COMMIT
- No skipped phases without blocked_by reason
- Coverage maintained or improved

# OUTCOME_RECORDING

    PYTHONPATH=$HOME/.claude/lib/python python -m des.cli.log_phase \
      --project-dir docs/feature/workspace-repo-path \
      --step-id 01-04 \
      --phase {PHASE_NAME} \
      --status EXECUTED \
      --data PASS

For SKIPPED phases:

    PYTHONPATH=$HOME/.claude/lib/python python -m des.cli.log_phase \
      --project-dir docs/feature/workspace-repo-path \
      --step-id 01-04 \
      --phase {PHASE_NAME} \
      --status SKIPPED \
      --data "NOT_APPLICABLE: reason"

# BOUNDARY_RULES
- Only modify files related to this step
- Do not load roadmap.yaml
- Do not modify execution-log.yaml structure (append only via CLI)

Target: 30 turns max. If approaching limit, COMMIT current progress.
If GREEN complete (all tests pass), MUST commit before returning.

# TASK
Apply RPP (Refactoring Priority Premise) levels L1-L4 to all files modified during the workspace-repo-path feature.

## RPP Levels
- **L1 Readability**: rename ambiguous variables, extract magic numbers, remove dead code, fix formatting
- **L2 Complexity**: simplify conditionals, extract long functions, reduce nesting
- **L3 Responsibilities**: ensure single responsibility, extract mixed concerns
- **L4 Abstractions**: improve type abstractions, extract common patterns

## Production Files
- app/src/server/workspace/validate-repo-path.ts
- app/src/server/workspace/workspace-routes.ts
- app/src/server/orchestrator/assignment-guard.ts
- app/src/server/orchestrator/routes.ts
- app/src/server/orchestrator/session-lifecycle.ts
- app/src/server/orchestrator/types.ts
- app/src/server/http/parsing.ts

- app/src/client/components/layout/WorkspaceGuard.tsx

- tests/unit/coding-agent-orchestrator/session-lifecycle.test.ts
- tests/acceptance/coding-agent-orchestrator/repo-path.test.ts

## Rules
- Read each file, apply L1 through L4 in sequence
- Do NOT over-engineer — only fix real code smells
- Do NOT add comments, docstrings, or type annotations where not needed
- Keep changes minimal and focused
- Run full test suite after each module refactored — all tests must stay green
- If no issues found at a level, skip it
- Commit refactoring changes with message: `refactor(workspace-repo-path): apply RPP L1-L4`

## Project Rules
- Never use null. Use undefined via optional properties
- Functional paradigm — pure functions, pipeline composition
- Do NOT create wrapper/helper functions for simple operations

1→---
     2→name: tdd-methodology
     3→description: Deep knowledge for Outside-In TDD - double-loop architecture, ATDD integration, port-to-port testing, walking skeletons, and test doubles policy
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Outside-In TDD Methodology
     7→
     8→## Double-Loop TDD Architecture
     9→
    10→Outer loop: ATDD/E2E Tests (customer view) - business requirements, hours-days to green.
    11→Inner loop: Unit Tests (developer view) - technical implementation, minutes to green, RED->GREEN->REFACTOR.
    12→
    13→Outer stays red while inner cycles. Outer drives WHAT to build, inner drives HOW.
    14→Never build components not needed by actual user scenarios.
    15→
    16→## Outside-In vs Inside-Out
    17→
    18→Inside-Out (Classic/bottom-up): discovers collaborators through refactoring. TDD guides design completely.
    19→Outside-In (London/top-down/mockist): knows collaborators upfront, mocks them, implements each moving inward.
    20→
    21→Use Outside-In when: architectural boundaries known (hexagonal), program to interface not implementation.
    22→
    23→## ATDD Integration (Lightweight)
    24→
    25→Original 2008 heavyweight ATDD was "too heavyweight for most real teams." Updated approach (Hendrickson 2024):
    26→- Few Given/When/Then examples, not many | Separate requirements from tests
    27→- Smallest subset of team with relevant skills | Value = shared understanding, not executable specs
    28→- Automate only where high-value
    29→
    30→## BDD Integration
    31→
    32→BDD emerged from Outside-In TDD. Given(context)->When(action)->Then(outcome) maps to outside-in mindset.
    33→BDD reframes TDD as design/specification technique, not just testing. More accessible to stakeholders.
    34→Gherkin: structured format bridging technical/non-technical. Use pragmatically - automate only where high value.
    35→
    36→## Outside-In Development Workflow (Bache)
    37→1. Write Guiding Test (acceptance) from user perspective - thick slice of functionality
    38→2. Start at top-level entry point, design collaborating classes incrementally
    39→3. Use mocks to experiment with interfaces/protocols
    40→4. As each layer implemented, move to previously mocked collaborators, TDD again
    41→5. Never build what isn't needed for actual user scenarios
    42→
    43→## Port-to-Port Testing
    44→
    45→Tests enter through driving port (application service / public API) and assert outcomes at driven port boundaries.
    46→Internal classes (entities, value objects, domain services) exercised indirectly - never instantiated directly in test code.
    47→
    48→Flow: Driving Port -> Application -> Domain -> Driven Port (mocked)
    49→
    50→```python
    51→def test_order_service_processes_payment():
    52→    # Setup - mock driven port (external dependency)
    53→    payment_gateway = MockPaymentGateway()
    54→    order_repo = InMemoryOrderRepository()
    55→
    56→    # Test through driving port (application service)
    57→    order_service = OrderService(payment_gateway, order_repo)
    58→    result = order_service.place_order(customer_id, items)
    59→
    60→    # Assert observable outcomes
    61→    assert result.is_confirmed()
    62→    payment_gateway.verify_charge_called(amount=100.00)
    63→```
    64→
    65→## Unit of Behavior (not Unit of Code)
    66→
    67→Test = story about the problem your code solves. Granularity related to stakeholder needs.
    68→A unit of behavior may span multiple classes. Test from driving port to driven port boundary.
    69→Key question: "Can you explain this test to a stakeholder?" If not, you're testing implementation details.
    70→
    71→## Classical vs Mockist Verification
    72→
    73→Classical TDD: real objects | state verification | less coupled to implementation | survives refactoring better.
    74→Mockist TDD: mocks for objects with behavior | behavior verification | lighter setup | more coupled to impl.
    75→Best practice: combine strategically. Behavior verification at layer boundaries, state verification within layers.
    76→
    77→## Test Doubles Taxonomy (Meszaros)
    78→- Dummy: passed but never used
    79→- Fake: working impl with shortcuts (in-memory DB)
    80→- Stub: predefined answers
    81→- Spy: stub that records interactions
    82→- Mock: pre-programmed with expectations for behavior verification
    83→
    84→Choose type by need: mock for interaction design | stub when don't care about interaction | fake for integration bridge.
    85→
    86→## Hexagonal Architecture Testing Strategy
    87→
    88→### Domain Layer
    89→Tested indirectly through driving port (application service) unit tests with real domain objects.
    90→Domain entities, value objects, domain services are implementation details. Testing them directly couples tests to internal structure.
    91→
    92→Exception: Standalone domain logic with complex algorithms (e.g., pricing engine) MAY be tested directly when complexity warrants it and the class has a stable public interface. This is the EXCEPTION, not the rule.
    93→
    94→### Application Layer
    95→Classical TDD within layer, Mockist TDD at port boundaries.
    96→Use real Order, Money, Customer objects in application service tests.
    97→Mock IPaymentGateway, IEmailService ports when testing orchestration.
    98→
    99→### Infrastructure Layer
   100→Integration tests ONLY - no unit tests for adapters.
   101→Mocking infrastructure inside an adapter test is testing the mock, not the adapter.
   102→Use real infrastructure (testcontainers, in-memory databases) to verify actual behavior.
   103→
   104→### E2E Tests
   105→Minimal mocking - only truly external systems (3rd party APIs beyond your control).
   106→Use real domain services, application services, repositories.
   107→
   108→## Test Doubles Policy
   109→
   110→Acceptable (port boundaries only):
   111→- `Mock<IPaymentGateway>` - external payment service port
   112→- `Mock<IEmailService>` - external email provider port
   113→- `InMemoryUserRepository` - fake for fast tests (implements IUserRepository port)
   114→
   115→Do not mock inside the hexagon:
   116→- Domain entities (Order, Customer) - use real objects
   117→- Value objects (Money, Email) - cheap to create, deterministic
   118→- Application services (OrderProcessor) - use real with mocked ports
   119→- Domain services (PricingService) - use real objects
   120→
   121→## Walking Skeleton Protocol
   122→
   123→At most one walking skeleton per new feature. When `is_walking_skeleton: true` in roadmap:
   124→- Write exactly ONE E2E/acceptance test proving end-to-end wiring
   125→- Implement thinnest possible slice - hardcoded values, minimal branching
   126→- Do NOT write unit tests - the E2E test IS the deliverable
   127→- Do NOT add error handling, edge cases, or validation
   128→- Skip inner TDD loop, go directly from RED_ACCEPTANCE to GREEN
   129→
   130→E2E tests are inherently slow and flaky. The walking skeleton is the ONE justified E2E test per feature.
   131→
   132→## E2E Test Management
   133→
   134→Enable ONE E2E test at a time to prevent commit blocks:
   135→1. All E2E tests except first one marked with skip/ignore
   136→2. Complete first scenario through Outside-In TDD
   137→3. Commit working implementation
   138→4. Enable next E2E test
   139→5. Repeat until all scenarios implemented
   140→
   141→## Step Method Pattern
   142→
   143→Step methods call production services, not test infrastructure:
   144→
   145→```csharp
   146→[When("business action occurs")]
   147→public async Task WhenBusinessActionOccurs()
   148→{
   149→    var service = _serviceProvider.GetRequiredService<IBusinessService>();
   150→    _result = await service.PerformBusinessActionAsync(_testData);
   151→}
   152→```
   153→
   154→Scaffold unimplemented collaborators with `NotImplementedException`:
   155→```csharp
   156→throw new NotImplementedException(
   157→    "Business capability not yet implemented - driven by outside-in TDD"
   158→);
   159→```
   160→
   161→## Business-Focused Testing
   162→
   163→### Unit Test Naming
   164→- Class pattern: `<DrivingPort>Should`
   165→- Method pattern: `<ExpectedOutcome>_When<SpecificBehavior>[_Given<Preconditions>]`
   166→- Example: `AccountServiceShould.IncreaseBalance_WhenDepositMade_GivenSufficientFunds`
   167→
   168→### Behavior Types
   169→- Command behavior: changes system state (Given-When-Then)
   170→- Query behavior: returns state projection (Given-Then)
   171→- Process behavior: orchestrates multiple commands/queries
   172→
   173→### Test Structure
   174→- Arrange: set up business context and test data
   175→- Act: perform business action
   176→- Assert: validate business outcome and state changes
   177→
   178→## Environment-Adaptive Testing
   179→- Local development: in-memory infrastructure for fast feedback (~100ms)
   180→- CI/CD pipeline: production-like infrastructure for integration validation (~2-5s)
   181→- Same scenarios: single source of truth across all environments
   182→

1→---
     2→name: quality-framework
     3→description: Quality gates - 11 commit readiness gates, build/test protocol, validation checkpoints, and quality metrics
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Quality Framework
     7→
     8→## Commit Readiness Gates (11)
     9→
    10→All pass before committing:
    11→
    12→1. Active acceptance test passes (not skipped, not ignored)
    13→2. All unit tests pass
    14→3. All integration tests pass
    15→4. All other enabled tests pass
    16→5. Code formatting validation passes
    17→6. Static analysis passes
    18→7. Build validation passes (all projects)
    19→8. No test skips in execution (ignores OK during progressive implementation)
    20→9. Test count within behavior budget
    21→10. No mocks inside hexagon
    22→11. Business language in tests verified
    23→
    24→Note: Reviewer approval (formerly Gate 12) and Testing Theater detection (formerly Gate 13) enforced at deliver-level Phase 4 (Adversarial Review via /nw:review), not per step.
    25→
    26→## Quality Gates by Category
    27→- **Architecture**: all layers touched | integration points validated | stack proven E2E | pipeline functional
    28→- **Implementation**: real functionality (not placeholders) | automated pipeline | happy path coverage | production patterns
    29→- **Business Value**: meaningful user value | testable AC | measurable success metrics
    30→- **Real Data**: golden masters present | edge cases tested | no silent errors | API assumptions documented
    31→- **Test Integrity**: every test falsifiable | behavioral assertions only | no circular verification | no mock-dominated tests | no assertion-free tests (see Testing Theater Self-Check in agent core)
    32→
    33→## Build and Test Protocol
    34→
    35→After every TDD cycle, Mikado leaf, or atomic transformation:
    36→
    37→```bash
    38→# 1. BUILD
    39→dotnet build --configuration Release --no-restore
    40→
    41→# 2. TEST
    42→dotnet test --configuration Release --no-build --verbosity minimal
    43→
    44→# 2.5. QUALITY VALIDATION (before committing)
    45→# - Edge cases tested (null, empty, malformed, boundary)
    46→# - No silent error handling (all errors logged/alerted)
    47→# - Real data golden masters included where applicable
    48→# - API assumptions documented
    49→
    50→# 3. COMMIT (if tests pass)
    51→# Use appropriate format below
    52→
    53→# 4. ROLLBACK (if tests fail)
    54→git reset --hard HEAD^  # Maintain 100% green discipline
    55→```
    56→
    57→For commit message formats, load the collaboration-and-handoffs skill.
    58→
    59→## Validation Checkpoints
    60→- **Pre-work**: all tests passing | code smell detection complete | execution plan created
    61→- **During work**: atomic transformation safety | 100% test pass rate | commit after each step | level sequence adherence
    62→- **Post-work**: quality metrics quantified | architectural compliance validated | test suite integrity maintained
    63→
    64→## Quality Metrics
    65→
    66→Track: cyclomatic complexity (reduction) | maintainability index (improvement) | technical debt ratio (reduction) | test coverage (maintenance) | test effectiveness (75-80% mutation kill rate at Phase 2.25) | code smells (systematic elimination across 22 types).
    67→
    68→For mutation testing integration, load the property-based-testing skill.
    69→

1→# Architecture Design: Workspace Repository Path
     2→
     3→## Problem
     4→
     5→The coding agent orchestrator creates git worktrees for task isolation, but `repoRoot` is hardcoded to `process.cwd()` in `start-server.ts:71`. This means:
     6→- Every workspace assumes the same repo (the server's working directory)
     7→- Multi-workspace setups pointing at different repos are impossible
     8→- There's no UI for the user to specify which repo a workspace operates on
     9→
    10→## Decision: Per-Workspace `repo_path`
    11→
    12→Store `repo_path` as an optional field on the workspace entity. Resolve it at assignment time instead of at server startup.
    13→
    14→### Quality Attributes
    15→
    16→| Attribute | Priority | How addressed |
    17→|-----------|----------|---------------|
    18→| Correctness | High | Each workspace targets the right repo |
    19→| Simplicity | High | Single field addition, follows existing patterns |
    20→| Fail-fast | High | Assignment guard rejects early if repo_path missing or invalid |
    21→
    22→## Component Boundaries
    23→
    24→This feature touches 6 existing layers. No new components are introduced.
    25→
    26→```
    27→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    28→│ Client                                                   │
    29→│                                                          │
    30→│  WorkspaceGuard.tsx ── repo_path text input (creation)   │
    31→│  use-workspace.ts  ── pass repo_path in create request   │
    32→│  AgentStatusSection ── show banner if repo_path missing  │
    33→│                                                          │
    34→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    35→│ Shared Contracts                                         │
    36→│                                                          │
    37→│  CreateWorkspaceRequest  ── add repoPath?: string        │
    38→│  WorkspaceBootstrapResponse ── add repoPath?: string     │
    39→│                                                          │
    40→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    41→│ Server: Workspace                                        │
    42→│                                                          │
    43→│  parsing.ts          ── validate repoPath in parser      │
    44→│  workspace-routes.ts ── persist repo_path on CREATE      │
    45→│                      ── new: validate-repo-path endpoint │
    46→│                      ── new: update-repo-path endpoint   │
    47→│                                                          │
    48→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    49→│ Server: Orchestrator                                     │
    50→│                                                          │
    51→│  assignment-guard.ts ── add REPO_PATH_REQUIRED check     │
    52→│  session-lifecycle.ts ── read repo_path from workspace   │
    53→│  routes.ts / wiring  ── remove static repoRoot, resolve  │
    54→│                         per-workspace from DB             │
    55→│                                                          │
    56→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    57→│ Schema                                                   │
    58→│                                                          │
    59→│  Migration 0018: DEFINE FIELD repo_path ON workspace     │
    60→│                  TYPE option<string>                      │
    61→│                                                          │
    62→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    63→│ Runtime                                                  │
    64→│                                                          │
    65→│  start-server.ts ── keep process.cwd() as fallback for   │
    66→│                     non-orchestrator routes only          │
    67→│                                                          │
    68→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    69→```
    70→
    71→## C4 Container Diagram
    72→
    73→```mermaid
    74→C4Container
    75→  title Workspace Repo Path - Container View
    76→
    77→  Person(user, "User", "Creates workspaces, assigns tasks to agents")
    78→
    79→  Container_Boundary(browser, "Browser") {
    80→    Component(guard, "WorkspaceGuard", "React", "Workspace creation form with repo_path input")
    81→    Component(agent_ui, "AgentStatusSection", "React", "Shows assign button or repo_path banner")
    82→  }
    83→
    84→  Container_Boundary(server, "Brain Server (Bun)") {
    85→    Component(ws_routes, "Workspace Routes", "TypeScript", "Create workspace, validate/update repo_path")
    86→    Component(orch_guard, "Assignment Guard", "TypeScript", "Validates repo_path set before assignment")
    87→    Component(orch_lifecycle, "Session Lifecycle", "TypeScript", "Reads repo_path from workspace for worktree ops")
    88→    Component(worktree, "Worktree Manager", "TypeScript", "git worktree add/remove/diff using repo_path")
    89→  }
    90→
    91→  ContainerDb(surreal, "SurrealDB", "workspace.repo_path")
    92→  Container_Ext(git, "Git CLI", "Worktree operations + repo validation")
    93→
    94→  Rel(user, guard, "Creates workspace with repo_path")
    95→  Rel(user, agent_ui, "Assigns task to agent")
    96→  Rel(guard, ws_routes, "POST /api/workspaces")
    97→  Rel(agent_ui, orch_guard, "POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/assign")
    98→  Rel(ws_routes, surreal, "CREATE workspace SET repo_path")
    99→  Rel(ws_routes, git, "git rev-parse --git-dir (validation)")
   100→  Rel(orch_guard, surreal, "SELECT repo_path FROM workspace")
   101→  Rel(orch_lifecycle, worktree, "createWorktree(repoPath, ...)")
   102→  Rel(worktree, git, "git worktree add/remove/diff")
   103→```
   104→
   105→## C4 Component Diagram: Data Flow
   106→
   107→```mermaid
   108→sequenceDiagram
   109→  participant U as User
   110→  participant UI as WorkspaceGuard
   111→  participant API as Workspace Routes
   112→  participant DB as SurrealDB
   113→  participant Git as Git CLI
   114→
   115→  Note over U,Git: Path 1: Set repo_path during workspace creation
   116→  U->>UI: Fill name + repo_path
   117→  UI->>API: POST /api/workspaces {name, repoPath}
   118→  API->>Git: git -C <path> rev-parse --git-dir
   119→  Git-->>API: exit 0 (valid)
   120→  API->>DB: CREATE workspace SET repo_path = $path
   121→  API-->>UI: 200 {workspaceId, ...}
   122→
   123→  Note over U,Git: Path 2: Set repo_path later (before first assignment)
   124→  U->>UI: Click "Assign Agent" (repo_path missing)
   125→  UI-->>U: Show "Set repository path" prompt
   126→  U->>API: POST /api/workspaces/:ws/repo-path {path}
   127→  API->>Git: git -C <path> rev-parse --git-dir
   128→  Git-->>API: exit 0
   129→  API->>DB: UPDATE workspace SET repo_path = $path
   130→  API-->>UI: 200 {valid: true}
   131→
   132→  Note over U,Git: Path 3: Assignment reads repo_path from workspace
   133→  U->>API: POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/assign {taskId}
   134→  API->>DB: SELECT repo_path FROM workspace:$ws
   135→  DB-->>API: repo_path = "/Users/marcus/myproject"
   136→  API->>Git: git worktree add (using workspace repo_path)
   137→```
   138→
   139→## Change Inventory
   140→
   141→### 1. Schema Migration (`0018_workspace_repo_path.surql`)
   142→
   143→```sql
   144→BEGIN TRANSACTION;
   145→DEFINE FIELD OVERWRITE repo_path ON workspace TYPE option<string>;
   146→COMMIT TRANSACTION;
   147→```
   148→
   149→### 2. Shared Contracts (`contracts.ts`)
   150→
   151→```typescript
   152→// Add to CreateWorkspaceRequest
   153→export type CreateWorkspaceRequest = {
   154→  name: string;
   155→  description?: string;
   156→  repoPath?: string;       // <-- new
   157→};
   158→
   159→// Add to WorkspaceBootstrapResponse
   160→export type WorkspaceBootstrapResponse = {
   161→  // ... existing fields ...
   162→  repoPath?: string;        // <-- new
   163→};
   164→```
   165→
   166→### 3. Server: Repo Path Validation
   167→
   168→New function in `workspace-routes.ts`:
   169→
   170→```typescript
   171→async function validateRepoPath(path: string, shellExec: ShellExec): Promise<boolean> {
   172→  const result = await shellExec("git", ["-C", path, "rev-parse", "--git-dir"], path);
   173→  return result.exitCode === 0;
   174→}
   175→```
   176→
   177→### 4. Server: New Endpoints
   178→
   179→| Method | Path | Purpose |
   180→|--------|------|---------|
   181→| `POST` | `/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/repo-path` | Set/update repo_path with validation |
   182→
   183→Request: `{ path: string }`
   184→Response: `{ valid: true }` or `400 { error: "..." }`
   185→
   186→### 5. Assignment Guard: New Check
   187→
   188→Add between step 3 (workspace membership) and step 4 (status eligibility):
   189→
   190→```
   191→3.5. Repo path configured — workspace.repo_path must be a non-empty string
   192→     Error: REPO_PATH_REQUIRED (400)
   193→```
   194→
   195→### 6. Orchestrator Wiring: Per-Workspace Resolution
   196→
   197→`wireOrchestratorRoutes` currently takes a static `repoRoot: string`. Change `createSession` to:
   198→1. Read `repo_path` from workspace record (already fetched in assignment guard)
   199→2. Pass it as `repoRoot` to session lifecycle
   200→3. Remove static `repoRoot` from `OrchestratorWiringDeps`
   201→
   202→### 7. UI: WorkspaceGuard Form
   203→
   204→Add text input for "Repository path" below description textarea. Optional, with placeholder showing example path format.
   205→
   206→### 8. UI: AgentStatusSection Banner
   207→
   208→When task is assignable but workspace has no `repo_path`, show:
   209→> "Set a repository path for this workspace before assigning tasks to agents."
   210→> [Set Repository Path] button
   211→
   212→## Risks
   213→
   214→| Risk | Mitigation |
   215→|------|------------|
   216→| Path becomes stale (repo moved/deleted) | Validate at assignment time, not just at save time |
   217→| Path traversal / injection | Validate path is absolute, exists, and passes `git rev-parse` |
   218→| Active sessions during path change | Block repo_path updates while any orchestrator session is active |
   219→

1→# Test Scenarios: Workspace Repository Path
     2→
     3→## Acceptance Test File
     4→
     5→`tests/acceptance/coding-agent-orchestrator/repo-path.test.ts`
     6→
     7→## Scenarios
     8→
     9→### Assignment Guard: repo_path Required
    10→
    11→| # | Scenario | Given | When | Then | Status |
    12→|---|----------|-------|------|------|--------|
    13→| 1 | Block assignment without repo_path | Workspace with no repo_path, ready task | Assign task to agent | 400 with "repo_path" error | @pending |
    14→| 2 | Allow assignment after repo_path set | Workspace with no repo_path, then set valid path | Assign task to agent | 200 with agentSessionId | @pending |
    15→
    16→### Workspace Creation: Optional repo_path
    17→
    18→| # | Scenario | Given | When | Then | Status |
    19→|---|----------|-------|------|------|--------|
    20→| 3 | Persist repo_path on creation | Valid git repo path | Create workspace with repoPath | Bootstrap includes repoPath | @pending |
    21→| 4 | Reject invalid repo_path on creation | Non-git directory path | Create workspace with repoPath | 400 error | @pending |
    22→| 5 | Allow creation without repo_path | No repoPath provided | Create workspace | 200, no repoPath in bootstrap | @pending |
    23→
    24→### Update repo_path
    25→
    26→| # | Scenario | Given | When | Then | Status |
    27→|---|----------|-------|------|------|--------|
    28→| 6 | Reject non-git path on update | Existing workspace | POST repo-path with non-git path | 400 error | @pending |
    29→| 7 | Reject nonexistent path on update | Existing workspace | POST repo-path with bad path | 400 error | @pending |
    30→| 8 | Accept valid git repo on update | Existing workspace | POST repo-path with valid git path | 200, bootstrap reflects path | @pending |
    31→
    32→## Unit Tests (to be created during DELIVER)
    33→
    34→| File | Coverage |
    35→|------|----------|
    36→| `tests/unit/coding-agent-orchestrator/assignment-guard.test.ts` | Add REPO_PATH_REQUIRED case |
    37→| `tests/unit/coding-agent-orchestrator/routes.test.ts` | Add repo-path endpoint tests |
    38→
    39→## Implementation Order
    40→
    41→1. Schema migration (0016)
    42→2. Assignment guard: REPO_PATH_REQUIRED check (unblocks scenario 1)
    43→3. Workspace creation: parse + persist repoPath (unblocks scenarios 3-5)
    44→4. Set repo-path endpoint (unblocks scenarios 6-8)
    45→5. Wire per-workspace repoRoot into orchestrator (unblocks scenario 2)
    46→6. UI: WorkspaceGuard form input + AgentStatusSection banner
    47→

input.content:
# Evolution: Workspace Repository Path

**Date:** 2026-03-07
**Feature:** workspace-repo-path
**Branch:** marcus-sa/coding-agent-orch
**Commits:** 0c17e23, 3682fa8, 8533174, f8ae64d, 2ed2973

## Problem Statement

The coding agent orchestrator hardcoded `repoRoot` to `process.cwd()` at server startup, forcing every workspace to operate against the same repository. Multi-workspace setups pointing at different repos were impossible, and there was no UI for specifying which repo a workspace targets.

## Solution Summary

Added a per-workspace `repo_path` field that is resolved at task assignment time instead of at server startup. The field is optional on workspace creation (allowing users to set it later), validated against `git rev-parse` to confirm a real git repository, and enforced by the assignment guard before any agent session can start.

No new components were introduced -- the feature threads through 6 existing layers: schema, shared contracts, workspace routes, orchestrator (guard + lifecycle + wiring), and client UI.

## Steps Completed

| Step | Name | Commit | Status |
|------|------|--------|--------|
| 01-01 | Schema migration + shared contracts | 0c17e23 | Done |
| 01-02 | Repo path validation + update endpoint | 3682fa8 | Done |
| 01-03 | Workspace creation with optional repo_path | (covered by 01-02) | Done (no new code needed) |
| 01-04 | Assignment guard + orchestrator wiring | f8ae64d | Done |
| 01-05 | UI: repo path input + missing-path banner | 8533174 | Done |
| refactor | RPP L1-L4 refactoring pass | 2ed2973 | Done |

## Files Changed

### Schema

- `schema/migrations/0016_workspace_repo_path.surql` -- `DEFINE FIELD OVERWRITE repo_path ON workspace TYPE option<string>`
- `schema/surreal-schema.surql` -- updated canonical schema

### Shared Contracts

- `app/src/shared/contracts.ts` -- added `repoPath?: string` to `CreateWorkspaceRequest` and `WorkspaceBootstrapResponse`

### Server: Workspace

- `app/src/server/workspace/validate-repo-path.ts` -- new `validateRepoPath()` function (shells out to `git rev-parse`)
- `app/src/server/workspace/workspace-routes.ts` -- `POST /api/workspaces/:ws/repo-path` endpoint; repo_path persistence on create
- `app/src/server/http/parsing.ts` -- parse repoPath from workspace creation request

### Server: Orchestrator

- `app/src/server/orchestrator/assignment-guard.ts` -- `REPO_PATH_REQUIRED` check before task assignment
- `app/src/server/orchestrator/session-lifecycle.ts` -- reads `repo_path` from workspace record at session start
- `app/src/server/orchestrator/routes.ts` -- wiring changes for per-workspace resolution
- `app/src/server/orchestrator/types.ts` -- removed static `repoRoot` from `OrchestratorWiringDeps`
- `app/src/server/runtime/start-server.ts` -- removed static repoRoot injection

### Client UI

- `app/src/client/components/layout/WorkspaceGuard.tsx` -- repo path text input on creation form
- `app/src/client/components/graph/AgentStatusSection.tsx` -- missing-path banner with action
- `app/src/client/hooks/use-workspace.ts` -- pass repoPath in create request
- `app/src/client/router.tsx` -- routing support
- `app/src/client/stores/workspace-state.ts` -- workspace state includes repoPath

## Key Decisions

1. **Optional on creation, required at assignment** -- Users can create a workspace without a repo path and set it later, but the assignment guard enforces it before any agent session starts. This supports exploratory workspace creation without blocking on repo selection.

2. **Validate via `git rev-parse`** -- Path validation shells out to `git -C <path> rev-parse --git-dir` rather than checking for `.git` directory existence. This correctly handles bare repos and worktrees.

3. **Per-workspace resolution at assignment time** -- `repo_path` is read from the workspace record during task assignment, not cached at server startup. This means path changes take effect immediately without server restart.

4. **Step 01-03 folded into 01-02** -- Workspace creation with optional repo_path was fully satisfied by the parsing/validation/persistence work in step 01-02, requiring no additional code.

## Test Coverage

### Acceptance Tests

- `tests/acceptance/coding-agent-orchestrator/repo-path.test.ts` -- 8 scenarios covering assignment guard (block without path, allow after set), workspace creation (persist, reject invalid, allow without), and update (reject non-git, reject nonexistent, accept valid)

content.text:
# NW-DISCUSS: Jobs-to-be-Done Analysis, UX Journey Design, and Requirements Gathering

**Wave**: DISCUSS (wave 2 of 6) | **Agent**: Luna (nw-product-owner) | **Command**: `/nw:discuss`

## Overview

Execute DISCUSS wave through Luna's integrated workflow: JTBD analysis|UX journey discovery|emotional arc design|shared artifact tracking|requirements gathering|user story creation|acceptance criteria definition. Luna uncovers jobs users accomplish, maps to journeys and requirements, handles complete lifecycle from user motivations through DoR-validated stories ready for DESIGN. Establishes ATDD foundation.

For greenfield projects (no src/ code, no docs/feature/ history), Luna proposes Walking Skeleton as Feature 0.

## Interactive Decision Points

### Decision 1: Feature Type
**Question**: What type of feature is this?
**Options**:
1. User-facing -- UI/UX functionality visible to end users
2. Backend -- APIs, services, data processing
3. Infrastructure -- DevOps, CI/CD, tooling
4. Cross-cutting -- Spans multiple layers (auth, logging, etc.)
5. Other -- user provides custom input

### Decision 2: Walking Skeleton
**Question**: Should we start with a walking skeleton?
**Options**:
1. Yes -- recommended for greenfield projects
2. Depends -- brownfield; Luna evaluates existing structure first
3. No -- feature is isolated enough to skip

### Decision 3: UX Research Depth
**Question**: Priority for UX research depth?
**Options**:
1. Lightweight -- quick journey map, focus on happy path
2. Comprehensive -- full experience mapping with emotional arcs
3. Deep-dive -- extensive user research, multiple personas, edge cases

### Decision 4: Number of User Jobs
**Question**: How many distinct jobs are your users trying to accomplish with this feature?
**Options**:
1. Single job -- one primary job to focus on
2. Multiple jobs (2-4) -- several related jobs, will need opportunity scoring
3. Unclear -- Luna will help discover and define the jobs through interview

## Context Files Required

- docs/project-brief.md | docs/stakeholders.yaml | docs/architecture/constraints.md

## Previous Artifacts (Wave Handoff)

- docs/discovery/problem-validation.md | opportunity-tree.md | lean-canvas.md — From DISCOVER

## Agent Invocation

@nw-product-owner

Execute *jtbd-analysis for {feature-name}, then *journey informed by JTBD artifacts, then *gather-requirements informed by both.

Context files: see Context Files Required and Previous Artifacts above.

**Configuration:**
- format: visual | yaml | gherkin | all (default: all)
- research_depth: {Decision 3} | interactive: high | output_format: markdown
- elicitation_depth: comprehensive | feature_type: {Decision 1}
- walking_skeleton: {Decision 2} | job_count: {Decision 4}
- output_directory: docs/ux/{epic}/ (JTBD + journeys), docs/requirements/ (stories)

**Phase 1 -- Jobs-to-be-Done Analysis (REQUIRED):**

Grounds all subsequent artifacts in real user motivations.

1. **Job Discovery**: Ask user what users are trying to accomplish. Capture in job story format: "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]."
2. **Job Dimensions**: For each job — functional (practical task)|emotional (desired feeling)|social (desired perception)
3. **Four Forces Analysis**: For each primary job:
   - **Push** (current frustration): "What frustrated users enough to request this?"
   - **Pull** (desired future): "What could they do that they cannot now?"
   - **Anxiety** (adoption concerns): "What concerns about adopting this?"
   - **Habit** (current behavior): "What behavior must change?"
   If interview transcripts|support tickets|analytics exist, extract forces from those instead of relying solely on user description.
4. **Opportunity Scoring** (multiple jobs): Rank by importance vs. satisfaction gap. High importance + low satisfaction = strongest opportunities. Produce scored table.
5. **JTBD-to-Story Bridge**: Each job story feeds into user stories and acceptance criteria in Phase 3. Every user story must trace to at least one job.

| Artifact | Path |
|----------|------|
| Job Stories | `docs/ux/{epic}/jtbd-job-stories.md` |
| Four Forces | `docs/ux/{epic}/jtbd-four-forces.md` |
| Opportunity Scores | `docs/ux/{epic}/jtbd-opportunity-scores.md` (when multiple jobs) |

**Phase 2 -- Journey Design:**

Luna runs deep discovery (mental model|emotional arc|shared artifacts|error paths) informed by JTBD, produces visual journey + YAML schema + Gherkin scenarios. Each journey maps to one or more identified jobs.

| Artifact | Path |
|----------|------|
| Visual Journey | `docs/ux/{epic}/journey-{name}-visual.md` |
| Journey Schema | `docs/ux/{epic}/journey-{name}.yaml` |
| Gherkin Scenarios | `docs/ux/{epic}/journey-{name}.feature` |
| Artifact Registry | `docs/ux/{epic}/shared-artifacts-registry.md` |

**Phase 3 -- Requirements and User Stories:**

Luna crafts LeanUX stories informed by JTBD + journey artifacts. Every story traces to at least one job story. Validates against DoR, invokes peer review, prepares handoff.

| Artifact | Path |
|----------|------|
| Requirements | `docs/requirements/requirements.md` |
| User Stories | `docs/requirements/user-stories.md` |
| Acceptance Criteria | `docs/requirements/acceptance-criteria.md` |
| DoR Checklist | `docs/requirements/dor-checklist.md` |

## Success Criteria

- [ ] JTBD analysis complete: all jobs in job story format
- [ ] Job dimensions identified: functional|emotional|social per job
- [ ] Four Forces mapped per job (push|pull|anxiety|habit)
- [ ] Opportunity scores produced (when multiple jobs)
- [ ] UX journey map with emotional arcs and shared artifacts
- [ ] Every journey maps to at least one job
- [ ] Discovery complete: user mental model understood, no vague steps
- [ ] Happy path defined: all steps start-to-goal with expected outputs
- [ ] Emotional arc coherent: confidence builds progressively
- [ ] Shared artifacts tracked: every ${variable} has single documented source
- [ ] Requirements completeness score > 0.95
- [ ] Every user story traces to at least one job story
- [ ] All acceptance criteria testable
- [ ] DoR passed: all 8 items validated with evidence
- [ ] Peer review approved
- [ ] Handoff accepted by nw-solution-architect (DESIGN wave)

## Next Wave

**Handoff To**: nw-solution-architect (DESIGN wave)
**Deliverables**: JTBD artifacts (job stories|four forces|opportunity scores) + journey artifacts (visual|YAML|Gherkin|artifact registry) + requirements (stories|acceptance criteria|DoR validation|peer review)

## Expected Outputs

```
docs/ux/{epic}/
  jtbd-job-stories.md
  jtbd-four-forces.md
  jtbd-opportunity-scores.md    (when multiple jobs)
  journey-{name}-visual.md
  journey-{name}.yaml
  journey-{name}.feature
  shared-artifacts-registry.md

docs/requirements/
  requirements.md
  user-stories.md               (each story traces to a job)
  acceptance-criteria.md
  dor-checklist.md
```

## Examples

### Example 1: User-facing feature with comprehensive UX research
```
/nw:discuss first-time-setup
```
Orchestrator asks Decision 1-4. User selects "User-facing", "No skeleton", "Comprehensive", "Multiple jobs". Luna starts with JTBD analysis: discovers jobs like "When I first open the app, I want to feel productive immediately, so I can justify the purchase." Maps four forces for each job. Scores opportunities. Then runs journey discovery informed by JTBD, produces visual journey + YAML + Gherkin. Finally crafts stories where each traces to a job, validates DoR, and prepares handoff.

### Example 2: JTBD-only invocation
```
/nw:discuss --phase=jtbd onboarding-flow
```
Runs only Luna's JTBD analysis phase (job discovery + dimensions + four forces + opportunity scoring). Produces JTBD artifacts without proceeding to journey design or requirements. Useful for early discovery when you need to understand user motivations before committing to UX design.

### Example 3: Journey-only invocation
```
/nw:discuss --phase=journey release-nwave
```
Runs only Luna's journey design phases (discovery + visualization + coherence validation). Produces journey artifacts without proceeding to requirements crafting. Useful when JTBD is already done and journey design needs standalone iteration.

### Example 4: Requirements-only invocation
```
/nw:discuss --phase=requirements new-plugin-system
```
Runs only Luna's requirements phases (gathering + crafting + DoR validation). Assumes JTBD and journey artifacts already exist or are not needed (e.g., backend feature).

input.command:
gh issue create --title "Set up shadcn/ui component library" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Context

The codebase currently has no component library or modal system. When implementing the workspace repo path banner (commit f2659f2), we had to use an inline form instead of a modal because no dialog primitive exists.

## Problem

UI components are built ad-hoc with raw HTML elements and custom CSS classes. As the product grows, this leads to:
- Inconsistent styling and behavior across components
- Reimplementing common patterns (modals, dropdowns, forms, toasts)
- No accessible primitives (focus traps, keyboard navigation, ARIA)

## Proposal

Set up [shadcn/ui](https://ui.shadcn.com/) which provides:
- Copy-paste Radix UI primitives (Dialog, Popover, Select, etc.)
- Tailwind CSS styling with full customization
- Accessible by default
- No runtime dependency — components live in the codebase

## Immediate unlocks

- `Dialog` component for repo path configuration and other settings
- `Button` with consistent variants
- `Input` with validation states
- `Alert` / `Toast` for error feedback

## Notes

- Uses React + Tailwind (already in our stack direction)
- May require adding Tailwind CSS if not already configured
EOF
)"
